metaTitle: The Boeing Atmosphere Global Documentation Hub - Patterns - Data visualization

header.title: Data visualization
header.description: Present complex data in charts and graphs

sections.text:
Guidelines

To help users quickly analyze complex data, you can visualize it in charts, maps, and diagrams.

Atmosphere doesn't have a component library for data visualizations. If your app includes data visualizations, Chart.js is the preferred chart provider.

Best practices

  • Always use Atmosphere's graph color palette, but never rely solely on color to communicate data
  • To avoid cluttering the interface and make the data easy to understand, limit the number of data points per graph

Pie or doughnut charts

A pie or doughnut chart shows numerical proportions. The size of each slice corresponds to the quantity it represents, which makes it easy to compare each part to the whole.

Use a pie or doughnut chart to display simple data sets with a limited number of categories.

Limit pie charts to a maximum of six categories.

images.text: To optimize accessibility, add labels, a legend, and values
Never add too many categories, as they can become difficult to interpret

sections.text:
Bar charts

A bar chart represents data in horizontal or vertical bars.

The height or length of each bar is proportional to the value it represents.

Use bar charts in the following cases:

  • To compare different categories
  • To track changes over time when the categories are discrete

Limit bar charts to a maximum of twelve categories.

images.text: To optimize accessibility, add labels, a legend, and values
Don't use different colors for data of the same category

sections.text:
Line charts

A line chart displays information as a series of data points connected by straight line segments. Each point on the line represents a value at a specific point in time or for a specific category.

Use line charts in the following cases:

  • To compare trends, patterns, and fluctuations in data
  • To compare different data sets on the same graph

Limit line charts to a maximum of four datasets.

images.text: Never include too many datasets, as they can become difficult to interpret

sections.text:
Background and secondary colors

To optimize accessibility, the color palette for charts is based on the foundation.ui.background.high background token.

Secondary colors

If you need to use secondary background colors for specific chart types, such as progress charts, then use the following colors:

  • Light mode - Space 90 and Space 99
  • Dark mode - Space 35 and Space 15

Glass material backgrounds

You can use glass material for chart backgrounds. Follow these best practices:

  • Always check that the glass thickness ensures accessibility
  • Evaluate whether a glass background is appropriate—for heavy data sets, glass material might clutter the view or take longer to load

Data types

Sequential data

When the order of data points is significant, the data is sequential. Sequential data steps are represented in a single hue, and the lightness of the color indicates a range.

The Atmosphere color palette for charts doesn't support sequential data yet

Divergent data

When groups or categories deviate from a common point or baseline, the data is divergent. To represent divergent data, color is used to indicate the distance from the center.

The Atmosphere color palette for charts doesn't support divergent data yet

Categorical data

Categorical data consists of values that represent distinct categories or groups. Categories are typically qualitative without a natural order or ranking. Color is used to visually distinguish each category.

Categorical color pallette

The categorical color palette uses a subset of the Atmosphere color system that was chosen after extensive research. To create a visual distinction between categories, the selected colors maximize contrast between adjacent colors.

Always follow the color sequences described in this section

sections.textAfter: Categorical palette sequence for light and dark modes

sections.text:
Color groups

If the number of categories is fixed, you can use the following color sequences:

Accessibility guidelines charts

The Atmosphere color palette for categorical data was carefully designed to be colorblind-friendly. To pass accessibility tests, WCAG requires a 3:1 contrast ratio for the background and the graph colors and the adjacent graph colors.

Most colors in the palette meet the 3:1 contrast ratio with the background.

To optimize the accessibility of visualized data, WCAG recommends the following:

  • Show labels, values, and a legend
  • Optionally use dividers for extra contrast
  • Optionally add a background pattern

images.text: If possible, add the following elements: legend, labels, dividers and values
Never apply your own colors to categorical data

sections.textBefore:
Text accessibility

Use solid white (#FFFFFF) or solid black (#000000) tokens for text on colors in graphs. To make sure that your text is readable, use the following color scheme:
sections.textAfter: Contrast scores for black or white text on a color. Each combination has a WCAG-recommended contrast ratio of at least 4.5:1.
